articles.title: How to Prepare for a Tantric Massage Session
articles.h1: How to Prepare for a Tantric Massage Session
articles.meta_description: A short, practical guide to preparing for a tantric massage — what to eat, what to wear, what to ask, and what to skip in the hours before your session.
articles.excerpt: Most people overthink this. Preparing for a tantric session is more about what you don't do than what you do. Here's the short version.
articles.content_html:
Most people overthink this. Preparing for a tantric session is more about what you don't do than what you do. Here's the short version.

The night before

Sleep is more useful than any specific prep. If you can, get to bed a little earlier and skip the late-evening caffeine. A rested nervous system gets more out of a session than a wired one.

Two to three hours before

Eat something light — a small meal, not a feast, not nothing. Bodywork on an empty stomach is harder than people expect. Bodywork right after a big lunch is also harder than people expect. Aim for the middle.

Drink water. Hydrated tissue moves better under the hands. You don't need to chug a litre; a normal amount more than usual is plenty.

An hour before

Skip the alcohol. Skip the heavy caffeine. Both interfere with the parasympathetic 'drop' that a slow session is built around — caffeine keeps the system on alert, alcohol dulls interoception. Either one will make the session land less.

If you smoke, don't smoke right before. The smell on your clothes makes a small room feel smaller.

What to wear

Whatever you arrived in. You'll be in linens during the session, so the clothes you wear in are just transit. Comfortable is more useful than nice.

What to bring

Nothing specific. The studio provides linens, oils, water, and everything else you need. If you take any medications you'd want a specialist to know about, mention them at intake.

What to skip

You don't need to shower-and-shave-and-perfume. A normal level of clean is enough. Heavy perfume or cologne is actually counterproductive — it lingers in the room after you leave and changes the experience for the next person.

What to think about beforehand

One question is worth sitting with: what would I want to feel differently after this hour? The answer doesn't have to be deep. 'Less wound up,' 'less sore,' 'less tired,' or 'I don't know, just calmer' are all completely fine. The specialist will use whatever you say to shape the session.

If you're nervous

That's normal — most people are a little nervous before a first session. The intake conversation is designed to walk you through what's about to happen so by the time you're on the table, the surprise is mostly out of the way. If a particular concern is on your mind, ask. Specialists have heard it.

articles.title: Tantric Massage Etiquette: Tipping, Talking, and Time
articles.h1: Tantric Massage Etiquette: Tipping, Talking, and Time
articles.meta_description: Clear answers to the small-but-real questions about tantric massage etiquette — tipping, talking during the session, arriving late, and what to do afterwards.
articles.excerpt: Bodywork has its own quiet etiquette. None of it is hard, but no one explains it. Here are the answers to the small questions most clients are too polite to ask.
articles.content_html:
Bodywork has its own quiet etiquette. None of it is hard, but no one explains it. Here are the answers to the small questions most clients are too polite to ask.

Do I tip?

In the United States, yes — tipping a specialist is standard. The usual range is 15 to 20 percent of the session fee. Cash is appreciated and reaches the specialist immediately; card tips are fine and also reach them, just a little later.

If a specialist does something especially attentive (extra time, a particularly careful focus on something you mentioned), tipping closer to 20 percent is the way people express that.

Should I talk during the session?

Whatever you want. Some clients are quiet, some chat, some only speak to adjust pressure or temperature. All are normal. Most specialists follow your lead — if you go quiet, they go quiet; if you talk, they engage briefly and then settle the room again.

The one moment you should always speak up: if anything doesn't feel right. Pressure, focus, room temperature, the music, the angle of the headrest — anything. Specialists want this feedback; it's part of the job.

What if I arrive late?

Call the studio as soon as you know. Most studios cannot extend the session into the next client's time, so a late arrival usually means a shorter session for the same price. If you call early, the studio may be able to adjust.

For longer delays — more than ten or fifteen minutes — the studio may need to reschedule. This isn't punitive; it's the only way to give every client a full session.

Cancellations

Twenty-four hours' notice is standard. Studios block specific time for you; a no-show or last-minute cancel leaves that slot empty. Most studios charge the full session fee for no-shows and a partial fee (often 50%) for late cancels, though policies vary. Read the studio's policy when you book.

What if I fall asleep?

Common, and fine. A specialist will not be offended. If you snore, they may shift your head slightly to clear an airway; if you're deeply out, they'll usually finish the session quietly and let you wake on your own.

What if I have a strong emotional response?

Also common, also fine. Slow bodywork can surface things — sadness, relief, a wave of feeling you weren't expecting. The specialist will not make it a moment. They'll usually just keep the touch steady and let you have the response.

Afterwards

You get dressed, drink some water, settle the bill, and leave when you're ready. No specific exit ritual. Most studios will hand you a glass of water and give you a minute. If you're not ready to drive, take a minute longer.

Booking the next one

If a session worked, mention to the specialist. They appreciate hearing it, and it helps them tune future sessions. Rebooking can happen right there at reception or by phone later. Most regular clients fall into a rhythm of one session every two to three weeks.

articles.title: Tantric Massage for Couples: A Thoughtful Look
articles.h1: Tantric Massage for Couples: A Thoughtful Look
articles.meta_description: An honest look at what tantric massage can — and can't — do for couples. What 'couples sessions' typically involve, who they're for, and the alternative most studios recommend.
articles.excerpt: Couples tantric massage is one of the most-asked-about offerings in the niche. Here's a thoughtful look at what it actually is, what it isn't, and what most studios (including ours) recommend instead.
articles.content_html:
Couples tantric massage is one of the most-asked-about offerings in the niche. Here's a thoughtful look at what it actually is, what it isn't, and what most studios — including ours — recommend instead.

What 'couples tantric massage' usually means

Across the industry, the phrase is used in two very different ways.

The first is the side-by-side version: two specialists, two tables, one shared room. Each partner gets their own session at the same time. The shared part is the setting — the soft light, the music, the post-session moment — not the bodywork itself. This is closer to a couples spa experience than anything tantric in particular.

The second is the instructional version: one specialist works on one partner while the other observes, with the specialist explaining technique and pacing as they go. The point is for the watching partner to take some of that home — to bring slower, more attentive touch into their own life.

What it isn't

In neither case does a serious tantric studio offer a session where partners 'practise on each other' inside the studio with a specialist watching. That's a workshop format, and it belongs in workshops, not in a bodywork session.

What we recommend instead

The honest version: most couples who come to us asking about a couples session benefit more from two separate sessions, booked back-to-back, in adjoining rooms. Each person gets their own hour. Afterwards, you share the post-session quiet — water, a slow walk, a meal — and that's where the connective benefit usually lives.

This is also less expensive than a true two-specialist couples session, and the bodywork itself is better because each specialist can give their full attention to one body.

What it's good for

That said, there are real reasons couples ask for shared sessions:

  • A shared milestone. Anniversary, a difficult patch resolved, a long trip survived. The shared setting marks the moment in a way separate sessions don't.
  • One partner who's new to bodywork. Having a familiar person in the room — even on the next table — can make a first session easier.
  • A genuine interest in learning. The instructional format does what it claims: it gives one partner some hands to take home.

How to book

If you're considering a couples session, call the studio rather than booking through a form. Five minutes on the phone will clarify which version a particular studio offers, what's included, and whether it actually matches what you're looking for. At RelaxTouch, we're happy to talk through the options with you.

articles.title: How to Choose a Tantric Massage Specialist
articles.h1: How to Choose a Tantric Massage Specialist
articles.meta_description: A practical guide to choosing a tantric massage specialist — the questions to ask, the signs to look for on a website, and the small details that separate professional studios from everything else.
articles.excerpt: Tantric bodywork doesn't have one universal certification, so choosing a specialist is more about reading signals than checking credentials. Here's what to read for.
articles.content_html:
Tantric bodywork doesn't have one universal certification — there's no single national body, no single school. That means choosing a specialist is more about reading signals than checking credentials. Here's what to read for.

Start with the website

Three things tell you a lot before you ever call:

  • Specificity. A studio that lists session lengths, prices, styles, and what's included is signalling that this is a real business with a real practice. A studio whose entire site is poetic copy about 'awakening' tells you something else.
  • Setting. A real address. A published phone number. Listed hours. Photos that look like a studio, not a stock-photo bedroom.
  • Tone. A site that reads like a wellness practice (calm, factual, descriptive of what happens) is different from a site that reads like marketing (vague, suggestive, performance-oriented). Both have their audiences; only one is what most clients looking for tantric massage actually want.

Call before you book

Five minutes on the phone is worth more than another hour reading reviews. A few good questions:

  • Can you describe what a session typically looks like, start to finish? A serious specialist will give a clear, concrete answer.
  • How long have you been practising tantric bodywork, and where did you train? No single right answer, but you want to hear something specific. 'I trained at X school for two years, and have been practising for five' is a real answer; vague gestures are not.
  • What's the difference between what you offer and a regular massage? The answer should be about pacing, attention and full-body work — not about anything else.

Red flags

Things that should slow you down:

  • No published address. A 'discreet location, address sent on booking' setup is not a wellness studio.
  • Pressure to pay upfront in full, especially for longer packages. Most reputable studios charge per session, with at most a deposit.
  • Resistance to questions. A specialist who gets defensive when you ask about training, scope, or what a session includes is not the specialist you want.
  • Coded language or vague marketing that suggests the listed services aren't the actual offering. Wellness studios describe their practice plainly — if you're reading between the lines just to figure out what they actually do, that's a signal.

Green flags

Things that should reassure you:

  • A studio that says 'no' to certain things clearly. A practice that's confident in its scope is a practice that's been operating long enough to know what it does.
  • Willingness to talk before you book. Specialists who treat the first call as part of the relationship — rather than just a sales call — are usually the ones worth working with.
  • A clean, navigable website that describes the actual experience. Beautiful copy isn't a substitute for clear copy.

The single most useful filter

If you'd be willing to send a colleague to this studio, or willing to mention them in conversation by name, they're probably a fit. If something about the studio makes you want to keep it private even from people you'd normally tell — that's a signal. Listen to it.

articles.title: A Short History of Tantric Bodywork in the West
articles.h1: A Short History of Tantric Bodywork in the West
articles.meta_description: How tantric bodywork came west — a short, honest history of the practice from 1970s Esalen to the modern wellness studio, with the key figures and the messy bits.
articles.excerpt: Tantric bodywork as practised in modern Western studios is younger than most people think — about fifty years old, with a messy, well-documented origin story.
articles.content_html:
Tantric bodywork as practised in modern Western studios is younger than most people think — about fifty years old, with a messy, well-documented origin story. Knowing it makes the modern practice easier to understand.

The starting point: Esalen, 1970s

The Esalen Institute, perched on the cliffs of Big Sur, was the centre of the human-potential movement in the late 1960s and 1970s. It was where Western psychology met Eastern contemplative practice, with mixed results — some serious clinical work, some self-indulgent excess, plenty of both at the same time.

One of Esalen's enduring contributions was a style of long, slow, full-body massage that came to be called 'Esalen massage'. It wasn't tantric in any classical sense. But its pacing, its full-body focus, and its emphasis on presence over technique laid the groundwork for what would later be called tantric bodywork in the West.

The Mantak Chia era

In the 1980s, Mantak Chia — a teacher trained in Daoist internal practices — brought a vocabulary of energy, breath and slow touch into Western wellness circles. His books sold widely; his workshops trained a generation of specialists and therapists. Much of what we now call 'tantric bodywork' borrows directly from his framing, whether or not modern specialists credit it.

It's worth being honest: Chia's work isn't classical tantra. It's a synthesis, by a Daoist-trained teacher, presented to Western audiences with terminology drawn loosely from South Asian traditions. The result is its own thing — useful in its own right, but not what it sometimes claimed to be.

The Margot Anand wave

Around the same time, Margot Anand published a widely-influential book on tantric practice (1989), which sold over a million copies. Anand's framing was different: more about intimacy, slowness and emotional presence than about energy systems. Her workshops trained another generation of specialists, particularly in Europe and California.

Anand's contribution to the modern field is the emphasis on partner work, ritual and emotional safety. A lot of what feels like 'the way tantric bodywork is supposed to be' in the modern West traces to her.

The professionalisation, 2000s

By the mid-2000s, tantric bodywork in the West was being practised in a wide range of settings: workshops, private practices, retreat centres and the occasional independent studio. The serious end of the field began to professionalise — clearer codes of ethics, clearer scope of practice, and training programs with multi-year curricula.

The less serious end of the field went the other direction. The phrase 'tantric massage' became a search-engine label that meant whatever the listing wanted it to mean. This is the confusion modern clients still navigate.

The modern wellness studio

What we offer at RelaxTouch — and what a handful of similar studios across the country offer — is the professional, wellness-oriented end of this lineage. Slow, mindful, full-body bodywork, performed in a real studio, with clear scope and a real phone number. We don't claim to be classical tantra. We don't claim to be revolutionary. We just offer the hour, done well.
